chain_of_thought: Attempted to use Ghidra MCP to search for the native function Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum but the backend endpoint returned 404, so direct decompilation is unavailable for this assessment. I must therefore reason using only the symbolic stack trace and library map.

The first application-level native frame below any allocators/sanitizers is Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum in libUbikeyJni.so, which suggests the crash occurs during ASN.1 certificate parsing, likely while counting or indexing child nodes in an ASN.1 structure.

The call chain is Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum -> Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT -> Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT -> CERT_Load -> Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetOrg -> fuzz_one_input. This is a realistic certificate-handling path: CERT_Load presumably loads a certificate blob, Convert_* translate to X.509 internal structures, and Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum operates on ASN.1 tree nodes.

In typical TLS/PKI and ASN.1 code, functions like Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum are responsible for traversing ASN.1 trees based on length/child counts encoded directly in attacker-supplied data (e.g., a DER certificate). If these functions are not rigorously bounds-checked, malformed or extreme inputs can cause out-of-bounds access, invalid pointer dereferences, or integer overflows when computing child offsets.

The fuzz harness entry fuzz_one_input strongly suggests that the input to this path is treated as a raw fuzzed blob, likely mapped to what would be a certificate in real operation. In production usage, the same code will be reached when the app loads certificates from external or semi-trusted sources (e.g., smartcard, NFC token, server, or file). Thus, the ASN.1 values (lengths, number of children, tag sequences) are largely attacker-controlled in normal operation, not just under fuzzing.

The function names (CERT_Load, Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT, Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT, Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum) are not generic harness helpers; they are clearly application-supplied parsers. A crash in Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum, without any indication that the harness passed impossible arguments (like NULL where app code never would), points to a parsing bug rather than a harness artifact.

Because I cannot inspect the exact instruction or memcpy/memmove usage, I must infer the vulnerability class from the context. Common failure modes in ASN.1 child-count computation include: (1) trusting encoded child counts without verifying cumulative lengths against the enclosing sequence length (leading to OOB read/write), (2) integer overflow on length*count when computing offsets, and (3) walking past allocated nodes due to negative or very large indices. All of these are memory safety issues (CWE-787/CWE-190).

There is no evidence in the provided data of a deliberate abort/sanitizer assertion (e.g., scudo, malloc, or explicit abort). The crash is reported generically as Process Termination : Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum, meaning the top frame is this function, not an allocator. That further suggests an invalid memory access or similar bug in this function.

Regarding attacker control, JNI bridge Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetOrg likely accepts a certificate or raw data buffer from Java. The Java callgraph is not provided, but given the app context (a banking app using UbikeyJni), these certs or keys will often be sourced from external devices or remote endpoints. Even if Java passes a byte[] validated only superficially (e.g., non-null, minimal length), deep ASN.1 structure constraints (child counts, nested lengths) are unlikely to be fully revalidated in Java, leaving room for malformed but well-formed-length inputs to reach Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum.

The fuzz harness does not appear to do anything unrealistic besides supplying arbitrary bytes. There is no sign of it violating ownership conventions (e.g., double frees) or breaking JNI expectations (e.g., using freed arrays). The crash occurs well inside normal parsing code, not at a harness boundary. Thus, this should not be classified as an Env/Harness-only issue; rather, fuzzing has uncovered a likely real parsing vulnerability.

Exploitability: if Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum miscomputes indices or array bounds based on tainted ASN.1 fields, crafted certificates could cause out-of-bounds reads or writes in native memory. For a security-sensitive banking application, any memory corruption in cert parsing is high impact and could plausibly lead to RCE in a worst-case scenario, or at least reliable denial-of-service. Without instruction-level evidence, I cannot escalate to certain RCE, but the class fits memory safety with attacker-controlled input.

Given the strong contextual indicators (ASN.1 parsing function, certificate conversion, fuzz-discovered crash far from harness boundaries, attacker-controlled structured input) and absence of contrary evidence, I assess this as a likely genuine vulnerability, though with slightly less than absolute certainty due to the lack of decompilation or concrete crash type.

Severity is best labelled as high due to potential memory corruption or at least process-level DoS in a cryptographic/cert handling context, but not critical because RCE is not demonstrated. Confidence around 0.75-0.8 reflects that this is more than speculative but not fully confirmed without code view.
reasons: Crash occurs in an application ASN.1 parsing routine (Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum) within libUbikeyJni.so, not in the fuzz harness or allocator
Call chain (CERT_Load → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_* → Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum) indicates complex parsing of attacker-controlled certificate data
ASN.1 child-count computations are historically prone to out-of-bounds / integer overflow bugs when lengths and counts come from untrusted inputs
No indication that the crash is triggered solely by unrealistic harness behaviour; fuzzing is feeding data along a path the real app uses for certificate loading
recommendations: Manually decompile and review Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum and its callers to identify and fix any missing bounds checks on ASN.1 child counts and lengths

Add robust structural validation for ASN.1 sequences and child counts before iterating children or computing offsets, including checking against container lengths and guarding against integer overflow
Augment fuzzing to include structured ASN.1 inputs with sanitizers enabled (ASan/UBSan) to confirm and localize any remaining memory safety issues
assumptions: Certificate/ASN.1 data flowing into CERT_Load originates from attacker-controllable or semi-trusted sources in normal app operation
Java side (UbikeyJni.jGetOrg) does not fully validate internal ASN.1 structure, only basic parameters like non-null and overall length
Process termination at Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum reflects a memory safety fault (e.g., invalid access) rather than an intentional safe abort
limitations: Ghidra MCP failed (HTTP 404), so the exact instructions, buffer sizes, and validation logic in Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum and its callers could not be inspected
No concrete crash type (SIGSEGV vs abort vs assertion) or register/memory dump was provided
Java callgraph for UbikeyJni.jGetOrg and exact source of input data were not available, so attacker control is inferred from typical usage patterns rather than proven

chain_of_thought: Native stack shows crash in __memcpy called from Decode_DER_ASN1_ST → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT → CERT_Load → Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetOrg, with fuzz_one_input as harness entry, so the first app-level native frame below the allocator is Decode_DER_ASN1_ST in libUbikeyJni.so.

Tooling for Ghidra (list/search/decompile) is currently failing with HTTP 404, so I cannot inspect the actual implementations of Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT, CERT_Load, or the JNI function. Likewise, I can only see the Java signature com.ubikey.jni.UbikeyJni.jGetOrg(byte[]): String but not its body, so I can’t check for length validation in Java or JNI.

Given the function names and typical semantics, CERT_Load / Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT / Decode_DER_ASN1_ST clearly form a DER/ASN.1 certificate parsing pipeline. A memcpy-based crash inside Decode_DER_ASN1_ST strongly suggests out-of-bounds copy (incorrect length computation, integer overflow, or trusting length fields in ASN.1 structures) when decoding attacker-supplied DER data.

The JNI method jGetOrg(byte[]): String receives a raw byte array from Java, likely representing certificate/credential data. Under realistic app usage, this byte[] will ultimately originate from untrusted sources (network TLS certs, smart card/U2F token responses, or file input). The fuzzer is exercising this in exactly the way a real attacker could: arbitrary DER bytes passed to the parser. There is no evidence (and I can’t verify) that lengths are safely checked before memcpy; the existence of a memcpy crash argues the opposite.

Because __memcpy is the crashing frame rather than an explicit sanitizer abort, and it is reached through a custom ASN.1 decoder rather than a hardened library like BoringSSL/OpenSSL with bound checks, it is highly likely that the decoder computes an incorrect length or fails to verify buffer capacity before copying. That is a classic memory corruption vulnerability (CWE-787/788/190).

This is not a harness-only issue: the JNI function used by the fuzzer is a real exported method in libUbikeyJni.so used by the app. The harness simply replaces the normal Java caller but doesn’t give the decoder any impossible state; it merely provides arbitrary byte[] input, which is realistic. There is no indication that in production the same code would be shielded from malformed ASN.1 (X.509 parsing inherently deals with untrusted external certs).

Without decompiled code I cannot prove exact bounds checks or pointer arithmetic, but the combination of (1) memcpy crash, (2) ASN.1 decoding context, (3) attacker-controlled byte[] parameter, and (4) lack of any visible validation layers is strong evidence of a genuine, potentially exploitable memory-safety bug rather than a benign abort or environment misuse.
reasons: Crash in __memcpy within DER/ASN.1 decoding pipeline (Decode_DER_ASN1_ST → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT → CERT_Load), consistent with out-of-bounds copy based on malformed length fields.

JNI function Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetOrg accepts a byte[] from Java; under both fuzzing and realistic use this buffer is controllable by untrusted input (certs/tokens) and fed directly into native parsing code.

No evidence of explicit bounds checking before memcpy; crash indicates memory corruption rather than a controlled abort or harness-specific misuse, and the JNI entry is a real app method, not a synthetic harness-only API.
recommendations: Manually decompile Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT, and CERT_Load to locate all memcpy/memmove calls and introduce strict bounds checks on lengths and buffer sizes before copying.

Treat all DER/ASN.1 length fields as untrusted: validate them against the remaining input buffer and detect integer overflows when computing offsets/sizes; reject malformed structures early instead of proceeding with memcpy.

Add defensive checks in the JNI method jGetOrg to verify the input byte[] length against reasonable maximums before passing to native code, and consider switching to a well-audited ASN.1/X.509 library rather than custom decoders.
assumptions: The byte[] parameter to UbikeyJni.jGetOrg ultimately originates from untrusted external sources (certs, smartcard data, etc.).
Decode_DER_ASN1_ST is a custom or lightly wrapped decoder and not simply delegating fully to a hardened library with its own bounds checks.
The observed __memcpy crash reflects an out-of-bounds copy rather than a hardware or allocator anomaly.
limitations: Ghidra MCP calls to list/search/decompile functions in libUbikeyJni.so are failing (HTTP 404), so I cannot show exact code snippets or variable flows.
Jadx MCP only revealed the UbikeyJni.jGetOrg signature, not its implementation; I cannot confirm if Java performs preliminary length checks.
Exact crash registers, memcpy arguments, and concrete buffer sizes/addresses are not available, so exploitability assessment is based on typical patterns and naming context.

chain_of_thought: Stack shows crash in __memcpy called from Decode_DER_ASN1_ST → CERT_Load → Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetOrg → fuzz_one_input. First app-level native frame below libc is Decode_DER_ASN1_ST in libUbikeyJni.so, which is a DER ASN.1 decoder used by CERT_Load.

A memcpy-based crash inside a DER/ASN.1 decoding routine is a classic sign of out-of-bounds read/write due to trusting length/offset fields embedded in the DER stream. These fields are attacker-controlled if the certificate/DER blob comes from untrusted input (network, file, NFC, etc.).

JNI bridge jGetOrg likely takes some encoded certificate (or UBikey data) from Java, passes it into CERT_Load, which calls Decode_DER_ASN1_ST. There is no evidence of the fuzz harness doing anything impossible for the real app; fuzz_one_input stands in for arbitrary Java data. No allocator/sanitizer frame is noted: termination is in __memcpy itself, which strongly suggests the immediate faulting access is caused by invalid src/dst/len pointers or length.

Given the limited data, we cannot see the exact C code, but the function naming (Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, CERT_Load) plus memcpy crash is strongly consistent with missing or insufficient bounds checking of length/offset values while parsing a DER-encoded ASN.1 structure. DER parsers often walk a byte array using lengths taken from the data; if those are not validated against the total buffer length, memcpy can overrun. This is a memory corruption vulnerability, not just a logic error.

Because fuzzing triggered this via Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetOrg, the JNI path is reachable and accepts arbitrary bytes in practice; in the banking app context, untrusted input could be certificates received during TLS/PKI operations or external tokens. That makes the length/offset fields effectively attacker-controlled. Therefore this is highly likely a real memory-safety bug in libUbikeyJni.so, potentially exploitable (heap overflow or read past end).

We lack exact offsets and parameters, so we can’t assign CWE with absolute certainty, but the best match is CWE-787 (Out-of-bounds Write) or CWE-125 (Out-of-bounds Read). Since memcpy usually writes to a destination buffer, CWE-787 is more appropriate, with possible RCE depending on heap layout → classify severity as high. Confidence slightly <0.9 because we don’t see decompiled code, but naming and crash point are compelling.
reasons: Crash occurs in __memcpy inside Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, a DER ASN.1 decoder, which commonly uses attacker-controlled length fields from encoded data.
Call chain from JNI jGetOrg → CERT_Load → Decode_DER_ASN1_ST indicates native parsing of externally supplied certificate/DER data with insufficient internal bounds checks.
Memcpy-based termination strongly implies out-of-bounds memory access (corruption or invalid read) rather than a benign abort, and no evidence suggests this is a harness-only misuse.
recommendations: Review and harden Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and CERT_Load to validate all DER/ASN.1 length and offset fields against the total input buffer size before memcpy/memmove calls.
Add centralized bounds-check helpers and replace raw memcpy with safer wrappers that assert source and destination ranges.
Add targeted fuzzing and ASan builds for libUbikeyJni.so’s certificate/DER parsing paths to confirm and precisely localize the overflow condition.
assumptions: Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetOrg accepts or can be driven by attacker-controlled DER/certificate-like data in normal app operation.
Decode_DER_ASN1_ST relies directly on encoded length/offset fields without full bounds checking around all memcpy calls.
The crash in __memcpy is caused by invalid length or pointers from Decode_DER_ASN1_ST logic rather than a glibc/libc bug.
limitations: No decompiled C/assembly for Decode_DER_ASN1_ST or CERT_Load was available to inspect exact parameter flows.
Java-side argument types and validations for jGetOrg are not visible, so attacker control is inferred from typical usage and the fuzz harness.
Precise nature of the overflow (read vs write, heap vs stack) and exploitability level can’t be conclusively determined from the provided trace alone.
